accordion_sections.heading: Assessment
accordion_sections.html:
• Classify dizziness phenotype (vertigo vs presyncope vs disequilibrium).
  • Establish onset, duration, triggers, recurrence pattern.
  • Perform focused neurological and gait examination.
  • Assess hearing/tinnitus/aural symptoms.
  • Review medications and orthostatic factors.
  • Perform Hallpike where indicated and trained.

accordion_sections.heading: Primary care management
accordion_sections.html:
• Hallpike-positive BPPV: perform Epley/canalith repositioning.
  • Use vestibular suppressants short-term only in acute severe phase.
  • Provide safety advice and encourage graded mobilisation.
  • Arrange reassessment if not improving or diagnosis remains uncertain.

accordion_sections.heading: Who to refer
accordion_sections.html:
• Immediate same-day emergency referral: Sudden-onset dizziness/vertigo with focal neurological deficit.
  • Immediate same-day emergency referral: Acute vestibular syndrome not explained by BPPV/postural hypotension and no trained HINTS assessor available.
  • Immediate same-day emergency referral: Concerning central features (e.g., central-pattern nystagmus, new severe gait unsteadiness, acute new deafness with vestibular syndrome).
  • Immediate same-day emergency referral: Associated major neurological symptoms (diplopia, dysarthria/speech change, unilateral weakness/numbness, reduced consciousness, severe new headache).
  • Routine referral thresholds: BPPV persisting after appropriate repositioning manoeuvres.
  • Routine referral thresholds: Persistent dizziness/vertigo beyond ~6 weeks despite first-line care.

accordion_sections.heading: Who not to refer
accordion_sections.html: • Do not routinely refer low-risk patients meeting conservative-management criteria in the review draft.
